summary:
Ganweriwala is a large Harappan site in the Cholistan Desert of present-day Punjab, Pakistan, discovered by archaeologist M. R. Mughal during surface surveys in the 1970s. Covering approximately 80 hectares, it is estimated to have been one of the five largest cities of the [[event:1680|Indus Valley Civilization]] during its mature phase, rivaling Mohenjo-daro and [[event:1682|Harappa]] in extent. The site lies along what was once the bed of the now-dry Ghaggar-Hakra river system, which supported dense Harappan settlement during the third millennium BCE.

Ganweriwala has never been systematically excavated, limiting what is known about its internal organization. Surface finds of Harappan pottery, terracotta figurines, and stamp seals indicate it shared the material culture of the broader Indus civilization. The eventual abandonment of the site, like many Cholistan Harappan settlements, is believed to have been driven by the drying of the Ghaggar-Hakra channel, possibly linked to tectonic activity or regional climate change around 2000 BCE.

read_more_links.title: Lost River of the Indus - Ghaggar-Hakra
